tlos: TLO 1. Remember the requirements of the Performance Test Plan.

events.description: Basic overview of the four performance data group test plans as well as the reports that are due following the flight data collection: Air Data System Calibration Report, Model Validation Report, and Performance Phase Final Test Report.

tlos: TLO 1. Describe elementary fluid mechanics.
TLO 2. Explain viscous flows and how boundary layers affect flow and drag.
TLO 3. Determine airfoil performance in incompressible flow.
TLO 4. Describe differences between finite wings and infinite 2-D airfoils.
TLO 5. Describe differences between complete airplanes and finite wing analysis.
TLO 6. Use thrust required/thrust available and power required/power available charts to describe aircraft performance.
TLO 7. Describe the differences between incompressible, compressible, and hypersonic flow.
TLO 8. Calculate flow properties through shock waves and expansion fans.
TLO 9. Describe the properties of external flow in transonic and supersonic flow.
TLO 10. Describe the properties of internal flow in transonic and supersonic flow.
TLO 11. Explain design considerations for supersonic airfoils and wings.
TLO 12. Understand low L/D approach and landing flight characteristics using the T-38C aircraft as an airborne simulator.
TLO 13. Understand the effects of thrust, drag, and airspeed on low L/D flight characteristics and energy management.
TLO 14. Apply the test wing safety process to safely exceed flight manual limits.

events.description: Subsonic aerodynamic fundamentals, equations and terminology used throughout the USAF TPS curriculum; airfoil analysis, forces acting on the aircraft, pressure gradients and the Bernoulli equation, and drag coefficients; boundary layer separation and control; Mach and Reynolds numbers and pitching moments.

Subsonic aerodynamic fundamentals, equations and terminology used throughout the USAF TPS curriculum; airfoil analysis, forces acting on the aircraft, pressure gradients and the Bernoulli equation, and drag coefficients; boundary layer separation and control; Mach and Reynolds numbers and pitching moments.

Subsonic aerodynamic fundamentals, equations and terminology used throughout the USAF TPS curriculum; airfoil analysis, forces acting on the aircraft, pressure gradients and the Bernoulli equation, and drag coefficients; boundary layer separation and control; Mach and Reynolds numbers and pitching moments.

Compressible flow from transonic to hypersonic; shock waves; boundary layer control over supersonic wings; airfoil shapes, shapes of aircraft structures, and transitions from subsonic to supersonic flight; lift and drag approximations for supersonic airfoil sections; principles of hypersonic aerodynamics and aerothermodynamics and their effect on aerospace vehicles.

Compressible flow from transonic to hypersonic; shock waves; boundary layer control over supersonic wings; airfoil shapes, shapes of aircraft structures, and transitions from subsonic to supersonic flight; lift and drag approximations for supersonic airfoil sections; principles of hypersonic aerodynamics and aerothermodynamics and their effect on aerospace vehicles.

Compressible flow from transonic to hypersonic; shock waves; boundary layer control over supersonic wings; airfoil shapes, shapes of aircraft structures, and transitions from subsonic to supersonic flight; lift and drag approximations for supersonic airfoil sections; principles of hypersonic aerodynamics and aerothermodynamics and their effect on aerospace vehicles.

The objective of this mission is to familiarize the student with low L/D approach and landing flight characteristics using the T-38C aircraft as an airborne simulator. The profiles flown simulate low L/D landing pattern approaches for typical unpowered research aircraft such as the X-24B and the Space Shuttle.

The objective of this mission is to familiarize the student with low L/D approach and landing flight characteristics using the T-38C aircraft as an airborne simulator. The profiles flown simulate low L/D landing pattern approaches for typical unpowered research aircraft such as the X-24B and the Space Shuttle.

tlos: TLO 1. Explain modeling and simulation assumptions and caveats.
TLO 2. Implement modeling and simulation to manage and mitigate risk.
TLO 3. Implement program modeling and simulation requirements.
TLO 4. Develop modeling and simulation requirements.
TLO 5. Explain the reasons for standardizing flight test data.
TLO 6. Describe empirical standardization methods.
TLO 7. Describe the increment method of standardizing.
TLO 8. Describe the ratio method of standardizing.
TLO 9. Describe errors caused by standardizing.

events.description: Use of analysis, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), and Wind Tunnel testing to predict aircraft performance. Programmatic considerations for use of Modeling and Simulation (M&S). Theory for the development, validation, and use of lift curves and drag polars during flight testing of aerospace vehicles, discussion of steady state, quasi-steady state, and dynamic methods. Determining uncertainty and interpreting its effect on data. This course is ungraded for STC.

Use of analysis, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), and Wind Tunnel testing to predict aircraft performance. Programmatic considerations for use of Modeling and Simulation (M&S). Theory for the development, validation, and use of lift curves and drag polars during flight testing of aerospace vehicles, discussion of steady state, quasi-steady state, and dynamic methods. Determining uncertainty and interpreting its effect on data. This course is ungraded for STC.

Use of analysis, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), and Wind Tunnel testing to predict aircraft performance. Programmatic considerations for use of Modeling and Simulation (M&S). Theory for the development, validation, and use of lift curves and drag polars during flight testing of aerospace vehicles, discussion of steady state, quasi-steady state, and dynamic methods. Determining uncertainty and interpreting its effect on data. This course is ungraded for STC.
Reasons for standardizing data to standard conditions; defensible methods for correcting flight test data from test conditions to standard conditions.
CSO/P/RPA will receive a flight in the T-38 to practice the test methods used to collect lift and drag data at constant Mach numbers through stabilized and dynamic flight maneuvers.
CSO/P/RPA will receive a flight in the T-38 to practice the test methods used to collect lift and drag data at constant Mach numbers through stabilized and dynamic flight maneuvers.
All students will fly a data collection flight to gather data for their model validation report using their data group aircraft.

events.description: The lecture discusses requirements and grading criteria for the model validation report.
The lecture discusses requirements and grading criteria for the model validation report.

tlos: TLO 1. Remember properties of the Standard Atmosphere for flight test conditions
TLO 2. Understand how altitude, airspeed, and Mach number are measured
TLO 3. Apply air data corrections in a flight test scenario
TLO 4. Analyze air data from air data Flight Test Techniques.

events.description: Properties of the earth's atmosphere applicable to flight testing; definition of the U.S. Standard Atmosphere and its assumptions; Pitot-static instrumentation; all inputs, outputs and limitations associated with Pitot-static systems including the procedures for error computation and correction and conversion of test readings to U.S. Standard Day calibrations; reduction of test data from tower fly-bys to determine the position errors inherent in aircraft on-board Pitot-static systems.

Properties of the earth's atmosphere applicable to flight testing; definition of the U.S. Standard Atmosphere and its assumptions; Pitot-static instrumentation; all inputs, outputs and limitations associated with Pitot-static systems including the procedures for error computation and correction and conversion of test readings to U.S. Standard Day calibrations; reduction of test data from tower fly-bys to determine the position errors inherent in aircraft on-board Pitot-static systems.

Properties of the earth's atmosphere applicable to flight testing; definition of the U.S. Standard Atmosphere and its assumptions; Pitot-static instrumentation; all inputs, outputs and limitations associated with Pitot-static systems including the procedures for error computation and correction and conversion of test readings to U.S. Standard Day calibrations; reduction of test data from tower fly-bys to determine the position errors inherent in aircraft on-board Pitot-static systems.

Demonstrate and practice tower flyby flight test techniques used to test, evaluate and calibrate aircraft Pitot-static systems. The sortie allows a student to demonstrate the ability to plan and perform a mission in an unfamiliar aircraft and environment. Student pilots will fly the mission in their data group aircraft (T-38 or F-16), and ABM/CSO/FTE/RPA students will collect data in the tower.

prerequisites.name: Air Data System Calibration Report Lecture

events.description: Demonstrate and practice tower flyby flight test techniques used to test, evaluate and calibrate aircraft Pitot-static systems. The sortie allows a student to demonstrate the ability to plan and perform a mission in an unfamiliar aircraft and environment. Student pilots will fly the mission in their data group aircraft (T-38 or F-16), and ABM/CSO/FTE/RPA students will collect data in the tower.

prerequisites.name: Air Data System Calibration Report Lecture

events.eventName: Air Data System Calibration Report Lecture
events.description: Requirements for the air data systems calibration report and grading criteria are provided during the lecture. The report documents the Pitot static system of the data group's data aircraft and provides an opportunity for students to practice report writing skills.

Requirements for the air data systems calibration report and grading criteria are provided during the lecture. The report documents the Pitot static system of the data group's data aircraft and provides an opportunity for students to practice report writing skills.

tlos: TLO 1. Describe the fundamental principles of propulsion and aircraft engine design, including the core components, and performance characteristics.
TLO 2. Discuss the function and design considerations of key engine components including inlets, compressors, fans, combustors, turbines, afterburners, and nozzles.

TLO 3. Calculate turbine engine performance using cycle analysis techniques, comparing ideal and real engine cycles, and produce key performance parameters such as thrust, specific thrust, and thrust-specific fuel consumption.

TLO 4. Assess engine operability, stability, and control characteristics, recognizing performance trends, testing methodologies, and safety considerations in engine operation, including during ground and flight testing.

TLO 5. Examine emerging propulsion technologies and systems, including adaptive cycle engines, rotating detonation engines, and integrated hybrid propulsion systems, and predict their implications for future propulsion applications.

events.description: These blocks cover the fundamentals of propulsion with an emphasis on flight test applications. It begins with first-principles analysis and applies them to various propulsion types, with a primary focus on turbine engines. Key topics include engine components, cycle analysis, performance evaluation, operability, and control characteristics. Students will assess propulsion system limitations, failure modes, and advanced concepts, including emerging technologies and alternative propulsion methods. The course prepares students to analyze, evaluate, and test propulsion systems in a flight test environment using analytical methods and practical applications.

These blocks cover the fundamentals of propulsion with an emphasis on flight test applications. It begins with first-principles analysis and applies them to various propulsion types, with a primary focus on turbine engines. Key topics include engine components, cycle analysis, performance evaluation, operability, and control characteristics. Students will assess propulsion system limitations, failure modes, and advanced concepts, including emerging technologies and alternative propulsion methods. The course prepares students to analyze, evaluate, and test propulsion systems in a flight test environment using analytical methods and practical applications.

tlos: TLO 1. Understand how to measure aircraft maneuverability through the use of energy maneuverability techniques.
TLO 2. Demonstrate collection and analysis of cruise flight test data.

events.description: Equations that can be used to determine takeoff performance and to correct flight test data to standard conditions; equations to analyze ground roll and speed on landing, braking distances and the effects of reverse thrust, aerodynamic braking and runway surface conditions on landing; effects of atmospheric influences and variations in pilot technique at varying gross weights under various conditions; discussion of a variety of typical takeoff and landing tests accomplished at AFTC; braking, anti-skid and barrier testing. The brief will cover test techniques for performing takeoff and landing tests and gathering data for takeoff and landing ground roll distances; development of a method for reducing these data to standard day conditions; and safety considerations that apply. Though specific flights are not dedicated to collecting takeoff and landing data, it is expected that students will collect the data on all applicable sorties.

Equations that can be used to determine takeoff performance and to correct flight test data to standard conditions; equations to analyze ground roll and speed on landing, braking distances and the effects of reverse thrust, aerodynamic braking and runway surface conditions on landing; effects of atmospheric influences and variations in pilot technique at varying gross weights under various conditions; discussion of a variety of typical takeoff and landing tests accomplished at AFTC; braking, anti-skid and barrier testing. The brief will cover test techniques for performing takeoff and landing tests and gathering data for takeoff and landing ground roll distances; development of a method for reducing these data to standard day conditions; and safety considerations that apply. Though specific flights are not dedicated to collecting takeoff and landing data, it is expected that students will collect the data on all applicable sorties.

Theoretical background required to determine aircraft cruise performance; data reduction methods oriented toward providing cruise data in the format used at the AFTC; definition of the basic cruise assumption of level unaccelerated flight; development of lift and drag functional relationships based on subsonic aerodynamics in a form suitable for cruise performance analysis; determination of thrust and fuel flow parameters; derivation of the equations for range and endurance; determination of optimum fuel flows and RPM settings for cruise.

tlos: TLO 1. Draw and explain flight envelopes, free body diagrams, Ps diagrams, maneuverability diagrams, V-n diagrams, and performance comparisons.
TLO 2. Determine the appropriate FTTs to use to develop these diagrams.
TLO 3. Apply cruise performance theory to analyze aircraft cruise performance with acceptable uncertainty.
TLO 4. Describe Takeoff and Landing performance theory and methods used for standardization and controlling uncertainty.
TLO 5. Apply takeoff performance theory to analyze aircraft takeoff performance with acceptable uncertainty.

events.description: Relationships of various forces acting on an aircraft in flight; the simplification of equations for steady climb and descent; factors that affect optimum angle of climb, maximum rate of climb and time to climb; analysis of polar diagrams for energy management; analysis of the forces acting on an aircraft in a turn; factors affecting turn performance; computation of corrections to standard day; methods of testing turn performance for non-standard day atmospheric and non-standard weight conditions; derivation and explanation of the equations used to correct check climb, level acceleration, and turn performance data.

Relationships of various forces acting on an aircraft in flight; the simplification of equations for steady climb and descent; factors that affect optimum angle of climb, maximum rate of climb and time to climb; analysis of polar diagrams for energy management; analysis of the forces acting on an aircraft in a turn; factors affecting turn performance; computation of corrections to standard day; methods of testing turn performance for non-standard day atmospheric and non-standard weight conditions; derivation and explanation of the equations used to correct check climb, level acceleration, and turn performance data.

Relationships of various forces acting on an aircraft in flight; the simplification of equations for steady climb and descent; factors that affect optimum angle of climb, maximum rate of climb and time to climb; analysis of polar diagrams for energy management; analysis of the forces acting on an aircraft in a turn; factors affecting turn performance; computation of corrections to standard day; methods of testing turn performance for non-standard day atmospheric and non-standard weight conditions; derivation and explanation of the equations used to correct check climb, level acceleration, and turn performance data.

Student ABM/CSO/FTEs gain practice directing a flight test mission. The students will practice building test cards as part of the MIB and flight preparation. The IP will demonstrate and allow the student to practice the 1G level acceleration and deceleration methods of determining specific excess power. The sawtooth climb method for determining specific excess power will also be demonstrated. Students will practice techniques for determining sustained level turn performance.

Student ABM/CSO/FTEs gain practice directing a flight test mission. The students will practice building test cards as part of the MIB and flight preparation. The IP will demonstrate and allow the student to practice the 1G level acceleration and deceleration methods of determining specific excess power. The sawtooth climb method for determining specific excess power will also be demonstrated. Students will practice techniques for determining sustained level turn performance.

During the F-16 or T-38 flight, student pilots/RPA pilots will practice mission planning and conduct ground operations, takeoff data collection, trim shots, and spot landings. They will observe and practice FTTs for determining sustained level turn performance using stabilized load factor or stabilized airspeed turns. Students will practice FTTs for check climb and descent performance.

They will observe and practice FTTs for determining sustained level turn performance using stabilized load factor or stabilized airspeed turns. Students will practice FTTs for check climb and descent performance.

They will observe and practice FTTs for determining sustained level turn performance using stabilized load factor or stabilized airspeed turns. Students will practice FTTs for check climb and descent performance.

During the F-16 or T-38 flight, student pilots/RPA pilots will practice mission planning and conduct ground operations, takeoff data collection, trim shots, and spot landings. They will observe and practice FTTs for determining sustained level turn performance using stabilized load factor or stabilized airspeed turns. Students will practice FTTs for check climb and descent performance.

TLO 2. Demonstrate appropriate abilities to complete an aircraft performance flight test in their assigned flight test team member role.

events.eventName: C-12 Autopilot Flight Control System Flight MIB
events.description: Introduction to flying the C-12 as a simulated remotely piloted aircraft by use of the Autopilot Flight Control System and throttles to fly performance flight test techniques. Includes FTEs assigned as test conductors for ABM/CSO/RPA Performance Practical Exams.

prerequisites.name: Data Group Level Accel/Turn Perf Data Flights 3

events.eventName: C-12 Autopilot Flight Control System Flight
events.description: Introduction to flying the C-12 as a simulated remotely piloted aircraft by use of the Autopilot Flight Control System and throttles to fly performance flight test techniques. Includes FTEs assigned as test conductors for ABM/CSO/RPA Performance Practical Exams.

prerequisites.name: Data Group Level Accel/Turn Perf Data Flights 3

events.description: The objective of this practical is to evaluate each student CSO/RPA's ability: to operate as an effective test team member during an airborne test, to flight plan before and during the mission, to perform a representative cross section of performance flight test maneuvers and to manage area navigation during the mission. The students' post flight analysis of maneuvers is evaluated through an oral debrief and written report. FTEs are assigned as non-graded test conductors for this event and will attend the MIB in addition to conducting the flight.

prerequisites.name: C-12 Autopilot Flight Control System Flight MIB

events.description: Following the C-12 Performance Practical Exam flight, students will write individual reports using the provided template, answering the requirements spelled out in the C-12 CSO/RPA Performance Practical Exam Test Plan.

The objective of this practical is to evaluate each student's ability to operate as an effective test team member during an airborne test, to evaluate each pilot's ability to flight plan before and during the mission and perform a representative cross section of flight test maneuvers and area plan during the mission, and to evaluate each FTE's ability to perform as a test conductor on a performance flight test, to include mission planning, flying, analyzing and reporting the results. Student pilots will produce a daily report and student FTEs will produce a test report answering the objectives from the provided test plan following the sortie.

events.eventName: Performance Phase Final Test Oral Report Lecture
events.description: Students will present a group oral report on the results of their maneuver and takeoff performance flight tests. Students will learn about presenting flight test results in a briefing environment with an emphasis on slide design and briefing technique. Evaluation of data and results is also discussed.

events.eventName: Performance Phase Final Test Oral Report
events.description: Students will present a group oral report on the results of their maneuver and takeoff performance flight tests. Students will learn about presenting flight test results in a briefing environment with an emphasis on slide design and briefing technique. Evaluation of data and results is also discussed.
